title: Introducing a queen in a cage
subtitle: Getting a new queen accepted by an unfamiliar colony.
excerpt: A colony that has been queenless for a week or more is not automatically ready to accept a new queen. The cage introduction method protects a bought or raised queen for long enough that the bees accept her scent before she can be harmed.
sourceNotes: BBKA queen introduction guidance, bbka.org.uk. National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. Snelgrove, L. E., The Introduction of Queens (1931), standard UK reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small wire or plastic cage used to hold a queen safely in the hive while the colony adjusts to her pheromones. One end is plugged with candy that the bees eat through over two or three days.
When workers surround a queen in a tight ball, restricting her movement. A balled queen will die from heat and suffocation within minutes if not rescued.
The process by which a colony learns to recognise a new queen as their own through prolonged exposure to her scent while she is caged.

content.text: A
queen cage

gives a new queen time. The colony has her pheromone in the air for two or three days before she can emerge and be encountered in the open. By then the workers' instinct to treat her as a stranger is usually overridden by their instinct to protect a laying queen.
The cage method works when introducing a bought mated queen, a queen from another colony, or a newly mated queen into a colony that has developed
pheromone acceptance
of a different individual. Colonies queenless for less than twenty-four hours can sometimes accept a new queen directly. The cage is the safer route in any case.
Before the introduction

Twenty-four hours before the introduction, check the colony for queen cells and remove any that are capped or close to capping. A colony that still has viable queen cells will not accept a caged queen, they will ignore her and emerge their own.
The colony should be queenless, with no emergency cells at or near capping. The ideal window is four to six days after the colony lost its previous queen.
Placing the cage
Light the
smoker
and open the hive. Work calmly; the colony without a queen is often testy. Identify the central brood frame and push the adjacent frame a few centimetres to create a gap.

Check the cage before placing it. The candy end should be intact. If the cage has a cork at the candy end, remove the cork, the bees will eat through the candy over two to three days to release the queen. If the candy is already gone, the queen will be free immediately; delay placement.

Suspend the cage in the gap between two brood frames, candy end uppermost, mesh facing the frame face where bees can contact the queen. Wedge it in place so it cannot fall. If the cage has a hook or hanger, attach it to the top bar. Close up the hive.
Observe the cage from outside the hive about an hour after placement. Gentle fanning and small groups of bees through the mesh is normal. A tight ball of workers clustered around the cage indicates
balling
behaviour. If you see a ball, open the hive immediately, remove the cage, keep the queen safe, and try again twenty-four hours later after rechecking for emergency cells.
Checking the release

After three days, open the hive and check the cage. If the candy has been eaten through and the cage is empty, the queen has been released. Look for eggs in the nearby cells as evidence that she's alive and has started laying.

If the candy is intact after three days, the bees have not been eating at it. This can mean the queen has died in the cage, or that the colony has lost interest. Remove the cage, check the queen is alive, and consult whether the colony has found their own queen by another route.
After release

Leave the colony undisturbed for five to seven days after you believe the queen has been released. Then check for eggs. A solid laying pattern building across two or three frames is the confirmation that the introduction has succeeded.
